stem: A 4-year-old non-Indigenous, otherwise well boy presents with 18 hours of right ear pain following a viral URTI. He has had no previous episodes of otitis media and is up to date with immunisations including 13vPCV. His parents are happy to follow a 'wait and see' approach if it is safe and want to know how to monitor him.

data_table.Examination: Right tympanic membrane: bulging, erythematous, no perforation, no discharge; left tympanic membrane normal; not toxic, alert, playful
data_table.Investigations: None indicated; clinical diagnosis of acute otitis media (right)

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days plus regular analgesia at home
options.rationale: Watchful waiting with adequate analgesia is preferred for non-severe AOM in non-Indigenous children over 2 years with no high-risk features. (Source: eTG; RCH CPG: AOM)

options.text: Intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily for 3 days as a single short parenteral course
options.rationale: Parenteral ceftriaxone is reserved for AOM with severe systemic illness, complications, or oral failure; not appropriate first-line in a well child. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Regular paracetamol or ibuprofen and fill antibiotic script only if no improvement at 48 hours
options.rationale: AU eTG endorses safety-net or delayed antibiotic prescribing in non-Indigenous children over 2 with non-severe AOM; analgesia first plus filled script only if no improvement in 48 hours. (Source: eTG; RCH CPG: AOM)

options.text: Topical otic ciprofloxacin 0.3 percent drops twice daily for 7 days with no oral antibiotics
options.rationale: Topical antibiotics are for perforated AOM or otitis externa; the intact tympanic membrane here makes them inappropriate. (Source: eTG)

options.text: Refer urgently for myringotomy and grommet insertion within the next fortnight as outpatient
options.rationale: Grommets are considered for recurrent AOM (>= 3 episodes in 6 months or 4 in a year); a single episode does not warrant surgical referral. (Source: RCH CPG: AOM)

explanation.summary: Acute otitis media is one of the commonest paediatric infections. AU guidance (eTG, RCH) supports watchful waiting with adequate analgesia in non-Indigenous children over 2 with non-severe AOM (no toxicity, no high fever, no severe pain) because most resolve spontaneously and treatment risks (rash, antibiotic resistance, diarrhoea) outweigh small benefits. Antibiotics are indicated for: under 2 years bilateral, Indigenous children, severe symptoms or systemic illness, recurrent or complicated AOM. First-line antibiotic is amoxicillin 15 mg/kg TDS for 5 days (Indigenous: 7 days).
key_points: Watchful waiting with analgesia first-line for non-Indigenous over 2.
Antibiotic: amoxicillin 15 mg/kg TDS 5 days (Indigenous: 7 days).
Indications for immediate antibiotics: under 2 bilateral, Indigenous, severe symptoms, complication.
Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children: treat all AOM with antibiotics.
Grommet referral for recurrent disease or persistent effusion with hearing impairment.
explanation.pearls: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children have a 4-fold higher rate of chronic suppurative otitis media and warrant antibiotics for every episode of AOM with longer treatment duration; routine watchful waiting does not apply.

stem: A 10-week-old formula-fed infant is brought to the GP with 5 weeks of frequent posseting, recurrent crying with feeds and now 2 weeks of streaks of fresh blood in stools. The crying is worst during and after feeds. She is otherwise alert; weight gain has dropped from the 50th to the 25th centile. There is no family history of atopy.

data_table.Examination: Mild eczema on cheeks, abdomen soft non-distended, normal anal exam, mild fissure not seen; weight tracking off centile downwards
data_table.Investigations: Stool: blood streaks visible; no infection symptoms; clinical exam otherwise normal

lead_in: Which initial management strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Commence omeprazole 1 mg/kg daily and continue current formula with anti-reflux thickening
options.rationale: PPIs do not treat CMPA and rectal bleeding with off-centile weight is not explained by simple reflux; protein elimination is first-line. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

options.text: Switch to a soy-based infant formula for 4 weeks then reintroduce cow's milk gradually
options.rationale: Soy formula is not recommended for infants under 6 months due to cross-reactivity in around 14 percent of CMPA and high phytoestrogen load. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

options.text: Trial of extensively hydrolysed formula for 2 to 4 weeks with paediatric dietitian input
options.rationale: Non-IgE-mediated cow's milk protein allergy presents with reflux, irritability and rectal bleeding; AU first-line is eHF trial for 2 to 4 weeks; soy formula avoided in infants under 6 months. (Source: ASCIA cow's milk allergy 2023)

options.text: Refer urgently for paediatric colonoscopy to investigate the lower gastrointestinal bleeding now
options.rationale: Infant proctocolitis from CMPA is the commonest cause of small-volume rectal bleeding in this age; colonoscopy is reserved for ongoing bleeding despite elimination diet. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

options.text: Add probiotic Lactobacillus reuteri drops and review symptoms after 6 weeks of therapy
options.rationale: Probiotics may help colic but do not treat allergic proctocolitis; protein elimination is first-line for blood in stools. (Source: ASCIA 2023)

explanation.summary: Cow's milk protein allergy (CMPA) is the commonest food allergy in infancy. Non-IgE-mediated CMPA (the proctocolitis form) presents with reflux, crying and small-volume rectal bleeding in well-appearing infants. Diagnosis is by elimination trial: extensively hydrolysed formula for 2 to 4 weeks, with soy avoided under 6 months due to cross-reactivity and phytoestrogen load. Around 10 percent require amino-acid formula; reintroduction at 9 to 12 months using the AU milk ladder confirms tolerance. Most infants outgrow CMPA by age 3 to 5 years.
key_points: Non-IgE CMPA: reflux, crying, rectal bleeding, well-appearing infant.
First-line: extensively hydrolysed formula for 2 to 4 weeks.
Avoid soy formula under 6 months age.
Amino-acid formula for severe or eHF non-responders (around 10 percent).
Reintroduce via milk ladder at 9 to 12 months.
explanation.pearls: Breastfeeding mothers of infants with CMPA can usually continue to breastfeed - the maternal cow's milk protein elimination diet (no dairy, no soy) plus a calcium supplement is preferred over weaning to specialised formula.

sources.label: RCH CPG: Cow's milk protein allergy

stem: A 5-week-old male infant is brought to ED with fever measured at 38.6 degrees Celsius at home and again on arrival. He was born at term by normal vaginal birth with no maternal GBS risk factors and has been feeding normally until today. He has fed less in the last 6 hours. He appears alert in the parents' arms but is mildly tachypnoeic without distress.

data_table.Examination: Alert, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, no rash, chest clear, abdomen soft; no focal infection; capillary refill 2 s
data_table.Investigations: Not yet done; needs evaluation per RCH SBI guideline

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate plan?

options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and safety-net advice with paediatric review in 24 hours
options.rationale: All febrile infants under 3 months require full septic workup; outpatient management of fever in this age group is not safe. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant)

options.text: Take blood and urine cultures, lumbar puncture and commence IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin
options.rationale: AU first-line in fever under 6 weeks is full septic screen (FBC, CRP, blood culture, urine catheter sample, LP) plus empirical IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin pending cultures. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant; eTG)

options.text: Take blood cultures and CRP only and admit for 24 hours of observation without antibiotics
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are required in fever under 6 weeks even when the infant appears well; the consequences of missed bacterial meningitis are catastrophic. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant)

options.text: Perform urine bag sample only and commence oral amoxicillin awaiting outpatient review tomorrow
options.rationale: Urine bag samples have high contamination rates and oral antibiotics are inadequate; catheter or suprapubic urine plus IV antibiotics are required. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant)

options.text: Administer intramuscular ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg single dose and discharge home for review
options.rationale: Single-dose IM ceftriaxone is not adequate empirical therapy in suspected neonatal sepsis under 6 weeks; admission for IV combination therapy is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile infant)

explanation.summary: Fever in infants under 3 months carries a 7 to 12 percent risk of serious bacterial infection (UTI most common, then bacteraemia, then meningitis), with poor predictive value of clinical appearance. AU first-line is full septic screen (FBC, CRP, blood culture, catheter or SPA urine, lumbar puncture in under 6 weeks) plus empirical antibiotics. Under 6 weeks: benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin; 6 weeks to 3 months: cefotaxime or benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin. CSF PCR for HSV is added if seizures, hepatitis, or maternal genital herpes.
key_points: All under 3 months with fever >= 38 degrees: septic workup + admission.
Under 6 weeks: full septic screen including LP; benzylpenicillin + gentamicin IV.
6 weeks to 3 months: blood + urine + selective LP per risk; IV antibiotics.
Catheter or SPA urine, NOT bag samples.
Add IV aciclovir if HSV risk factors (seizures, transaminitis, maternal HSV).
explanation.pearls: A 'fever-of-unknown-source' infant under 90 days with a positive urine culture still needs a normal CSF sample before stopping presumptive meningitis cover - around 5 percent of febrile UTIs in this age have concomitant meningitis.

stem: A 2-year-old boy is admitted with 6 days of fever to 39.5 degrees Celsius. He has bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, cracked red lips, an erythematous polymorphous trunk rash and induration of the hands but no significant cervical lymphadenopathy. Screening for streptococcal infection and respiratory viruses has been negative. The team is uncertain whether he meets criteria for definite Kawasaki disease.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, cracked lips, no exudate, polymorphous rash, dorsal hand induration; no cervical lymphadenopathy >= 1.5 cm
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, platelets 525 x10^9/L, WCC 16 (neutrophils 12), CRP 145 mg/L, ESR 78 mm/h, ALT 84 U/L, albumin 28 g/L, urinalysis sterile pyuria; echo: small proximal LAD dilation (Z+2.1)

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics and reassess at 24 hours before further treatment
options.rationale: Sustained fever over 5 days with three principal features and supportive labs plus coronary dilation already meets incomplete Kawasaki criteria; delaying IVIG increases aneurysm risk. (Source: AHA Kawasaki 2017; RCH CPG: Kawasaki)

options.text: Administer IVIG 2 g/kg over 10 to 12 hours plus high-dose aspirin and arrange paediatric cardiology
options.rationale: Incomplete KD with fever >= 5 days plus 3 features plus supportive labs (CRP, neutrophilia, anaemia, thrombocytosis, raised ALT, hypoalbuminaemia) and coronary dilation justifies IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin to reduce aneurysm risk. (Source: AHA Kawasaki 2017; RCH CPG)

options.text: Start oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg daily as primary therapy and withhold IVIG until day 10
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are added (KAICA-style) for high-risk KD or IVIG-refractory disease; they are not primary therapy alone in standard initial care. (Source: AHA Kawasaki 2017)

options.text: Defer all treatment until thrombocytosis exceeds 600 x10^9/L and skin desquamation appears
options.rationale: Thrombocytosis and desquamation typically appear in week 2 to 3; waiting risks aneurysm formation - IVIG within 10 days reduces incidence from 25 percent to under 5 percent. (Source: AHA Kawasaki 2017)

options.text: Treat as scarlet fever with intravenous benzylpenicillin and reassess after 48 hours
options.rationale: Negative streptococcal screen and the clinical phenotype (conjunctivitis, mucositis, hand induration) plus coronary changes make Kawasaki the working diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Kawasaki)

explanation.summary: Kawasaki disease is a self-limited medium-vessel vasculitis with a strong predilection for coronary arteries. Classic diagnosis: fever >= 5 days plus 4 of 5 features (bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, mucositis, polymorphous rash, extremity changes, unilateral cervical lymphadenopathy). Incomplete KD = fever >= 5 days plus 2 to 3 features plus supportive labs (CRP >= 30, ESR >= 40, plus albumin under 30, anaemia, ALT raised, platelets >= 450 after 7 days, WCC >= 15, sterile pyuria) and/or coronary changes on echo. IVIG 2 g/kg within 10 days of fever reduces coronary aneurysm risk five-fold; aspirin is added for anti-inflammatory then antiplatelet effect.
key_points: Classic KD: fever >= 5 days plus 4 of 5 features.
Incomplete KD: 2 to 3 features plus supportive labs or coronary changes.
Treat within 10 days: IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin.
Supportive labs: CRP, ESR, neutrophilia, anaemia, thrombocytosis after day 7.
Echo at diagnosis, 2 weeks and 6 weeks to monitor coronaries.
explanation.pearls: An infant under 6 months with prolonged fever and irritability without classical KD features still warrants echocardiography because around 80 percent of KD-related coronary aneurysms in this age group present as incomplete or 'atypical' disease.

stem: A 6-year-old girl with focal epilepsy on valproate is brought to ED actively seizing. Paramedics gave IM midazolam 5 mg en route at 8 minutes and IV midazolam 0.15 mg/kg on arrival at 18 minutes, but generalised tonic-clonic activity has continued. It is now 28 minutes of continuous seizure. The team is preparing the second-line agent.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 178, RR 32 (ongoing seizure), SpO2 93% on 6 L oxygen mask, T 37.2°C, BSL 5.6 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Ongoing generalised tonic-clonic activity, eyes deviated upwards, pupils 4 mm equal; secured airway, IV access x 2
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.22, pCO2 56 mmHg, HCO3 19, lactate 5.8, Na 138, K 4.0, Mg 0.84, Ca 2.32

lead_in: Which second-line anticonvulsant is most appropriate?

options.text: Intravenous phenytoin 20 mg/kg loading at 1 mg/kg/min with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Phenytoin remains an acceptable option but carries higher cardiotoxicity and infusion-site risks; AU paediatric practice increasingly prefers levetiracetam. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Additional intravenous midazolam 0.15 mg/kg bolus then continuous infusion at 0.1 mg/kg/h
options.rationale: Further benzodiazepine after two failed doses increases respiratory depression without controlling seizure; second-line anticonvulsant is the correct step. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

options.text: Intravenous sodium valproate 40 mg/kg load over 10 minutes given her existing valproate use
options.rationale: Valproate is an option but not preferred when the patient is already on regular valproate (risk of overshoot toxicity); levetiracetam or phenytoin is preferred. (Source: RCH CPG)

options.text: Intravenous levetiracetam 60 mg/kg loading dose over 5 minutes with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: ConSEPT and EcLiPSE trials showed levetiracetam equivalent to phenytoin for paediatric status epilepticus and it has a more favourable side-effect profile, making it AU first-line second-line in many centres. (Source: ConSEPT/EcLiPSE; ANZICS paediatric)

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation and propofol infusion as primary management without anticonvulsant loading
options.rationale: Refractory status requires anaesthetic agents AFTER a second-line anticonvulsant has been loaded; jumping to thiopentone/propofol is a third-line step. (Source: RCH CPG: Status epilepticus)

explanation.summary: Paediatric convulsive status epilepticus follows a tiered approach: 0 to 5 minutes assess airway, oxygen, IV access; 5 to 20 minutes first benzodiazepine (IV midazolam 0.15 mg/kg or buccal 0.3 mg/kg); 20 to 40 minutes second benzodiazepine if still seizing; 30 to 40 minutes second-line anticonvulsant (levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, phenytoin 20 mg/kg, or valproate 40 mg/kg). Refractory status (> 40 minutes) requires intubation and a third-line agent (thiopentone, midazolam infusion or propofol) in PICU. Hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hypocalcaemia, hypomagnesaemia and pyridoxine deficiency must be excluded.
key_points: Two benzodiazepines then second-line anticonvulsant.
Second-line equivalence: levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, phenytoin 20 mg/kg, valproate 40 mg/kg.
Levetiracetam preferred in AU paediatric centres for safety.
Refractory status (> 40 min) = intubate + anaesthetic agent in PICU.
Exclude reversible causes: glucose, sodium, calcium, magnesium, pyridoxine, drugs.
explanation.pearls: Pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy presents under 18 months with apparently medication-refractory status; a single trial of pyridoxine 100 mg IV during ongoing seizures is diagnostic and therapeutic if there is rapid termination of seizure activity within minutes.
